Climbing equipment on site was unsecured and exposed to adverse conditions.
findings.recommendation: Erect a secure exclusion zone around the tower and post hazard signs immediately. Do not permit climbing until certified fall-protection anchors are installed and verified, and missing signage and barriers are replaced within 24 hours.

summary: The Harbor Relay Station is generally in good functional condition with all six antennas operational and the tower remaining plumb within acceptable tolerances. Several minor maintenance issues were observed that do not currently compromise structural integrity or communications coverage. Recommended actions are routine maintenance and targeted repairs to restore full compliance with manufacturer and TIA-222 guidance.

findings.description: Localized paint flaking and surface oxidation observed on the north leg between 25 and 30 m elevation, affecting approximately 8% of that leg's exposed surface. Average coating thickness measured 80 µm versus the recommended 100 µm for exterior corrosive environments.
findings.recommendation: Perform targeted surface preparation and touch-up coating in affected areas within 180 days and schedule a full exterior repaint of the tower within 12 months; monitor coating condition annually.

findings.description: Grounding conductor resistance measured at 7.5 Ω between the main bond and the grounding grid, exceeding the preferred threshold of <5 Ω for reliable lightning protection. Bonding connections at the baseplate show light surface corrosion but maintain physical continuity.
findings.recommendation: Repair/replace corroded bonding conductors and connections and re-test to achieve <5 Ω within 30 days; include grounding check in annual lightning protection maintenance.

findings.description: Fixed ladder and fall-protection cage are present and serviceable, though rungs 12–14 show moderate wear and one ladder rung cover is split. Personal-arrest anchor points are intact and certified sticker is current.
findings.recommendation: Replace worn rungs (12–14) and split covers within 90 days and perform quarterly inspections of climbway components.

summary: The Harbor Relay Station shows fair condition with several maintenance deficiencies that could progress to service-impairing failures if not addressed. Corrosion and mechanical loosening are the primary concerns and have produced measurable degradation at the base and select antenna mounts. Prioritized repairs are required to restore reliable operation and structural resiliency.

findings.description: Moderate to advanced corrosion noted at the base plate underside of the east leg and adjacent concrete interface; corrosion products and staining present. Measured thread section loss on two anchor bolts is approximately 18–20%, reducing effective thread engagement.
findings.recommendation: Schedule remediation within 30 days: remove corrosion, clean and apply approved corrosion inhibitor and protective coating to the base area, and replace anchor bolts with new bolts where section loss exceeds 15%.

findings.description: Antenna mount fasteners for antenna #4 (north face, 28 m elevation) show two bolts with torque readings of 30 Nm versus the specified 60 Nm, and slight movement detected under manual load. Adjacent mounts show no immediate looseness but should be verified.
findings.recommendation: Re-torque all antenna mount fasteners to specification and replace the two undersized/stripped bolts with grade 8.8 bolts within 14 days; inspect all antenna mounts quarterly thereafter.

findings.description: RF feed-through at the 32 m cable penetration exhibits dielectric degradation with elevated VSWR measured at 1.8:1 (acceptable target <1.5:1) and visible weathering of the feedline jacket. Signal loss remains within operational limits but is trending upward.
findings.recommendation: Replace the affected feed-through assembly and feedline segment within 30 days and perform a full RF sweep and VSWR verification post-repair.

findings.description: Surface water pooling approximately 10 mm deep observed around the base perimeter following recent precipitation, caused by partially clogged perimeter drainage channel. No standing water was observed inside the shelter.
findings.recommendation: Clear drainage channels and remove debris within 60 days; consider installing a perimeter gravel sump to improve drainage and review after next significant rainfall event.

summary: Significant deterioration at the Harbor Relay Station poses an immediate risk to structural integrity and safe operations. Measured foundation settlement, severe corrosion of lower members, and a failed grounding system require urgent action to prevent collapse or catastrophic failure. Recommend immediate access restrictions and emergency structural stabilization followed by comprehensive repairs.

findings.description: Observed lateral displacement at the tower apex of approximately 0.60 m (measured by tape and plumb reference), corresponding to an angular lean of ~1.0–1.7 degrees. Differential settlement of the east anchor point measured 35 mm relative to the west anchor; cracks and spalling noted in the east footing concrete.
findings.recommendation: Immediately restrict access to the tower and cease non-essential operations within 24 hours. Engage a licensed structural engineer for emergency stabilization within 72 hours and implement underpinning or shoring as directed; consider temporary removal of antenna payload during stabilization.

findings.description: Lower leg members between 2–6 m elevation (south-east leg) exhibit advanced section loss with localized perforation and gusset plate thinning up to 40%. Fasteners in this area are heavily corroded and several show loss of head integrity.
findings.recommendation: Do not resume climbing activity; replace the affected lower leg members and gusset plates per engineered repair drawings within 30 days or sooner as determined by the structural engineer. Isolate and brace the corroded members immediately to limit further degradation.

findings.description: Ground system failure: measured ground resistance of the station is 15 Ω (target <5 Ω) and the primary bonding conductor is severed approximately 0.5 m below grade. Lightning arrestor device for antenna #2 is missing and evidence of a prior strike is present on the feedline connector.
findings.recommendation: Perform emergency grounding repairs within 48 hours: install new driven ground rods, replace bonding conductor, and re-establish continuity to achieve <5 Ω. Install missing lightning arrestor on antenna #2 and retest the system immediately after repairs.

findings.description: Permanent fall-protection anchor points on the top working platform are corroded and do not meet pull-test requirements; top-platform handrails show significant corrosion and two sections are loose. No current restricted-area signage is posted at the perimeter gate.
findings.recommendation: Prohibit climbing and platform access immediately and post restricted-area signage within 24 hours. Install temporary certified anchor points and replace corroded handrail sections within 14 days; perform certified fall-protection inspections prior to restoring access.

summary: The Ironworks Water Treatment Plant is operating reliably with tertiary treatment processes meeting most performance criteria. Minor mechanical wear and isolated electrical concerns were observed that do not currently compromise effluent quality but warrant prompt maintenance to prevent degradation.

findings.description: Vibration readings on clarifier #2 drive bearing at the north edge measured 6.2 mm/s (overall velocity) which exceeds the facility guideline of 4.5 mm/s and indicates imbalance or bearing wear. Observed audible noise and lateral shaft play of ~1.5 mm during startup.
findings.recommendation: Schedule balancing and bearing inspection within 30 days and replace worn bearings if wear exceeds manufacturer tolerance; re-measure vibration after repair to verify reduction below 4.5 mm/s.

findings.description: Minor surface corrosion present on the sludge recirculation piping cluster (east mezzanine) with localized pitting up to 0.8 mm depth and flaking of the protective coating. Corrosion is currently superficial and not causing leakage.
findings.recommendation: Clean and recoat affected pipe sections during the next planned outage (within 90 days) and implement quarterly visual monitoring for progression.

findings.description: Battery bank for the on-site backup generator (generator B, control room A) showed a mean cell voltage of 1.95 V/cell under float, a 6% reduction from baseline commissioning values, indicating aging capacity. Start-up test showed generator transfer time within acceptable limit but reduced runtime in battery-backed controls.
findings.recommendation: Replace the battery bank within 180 days or sooner if load-testing demonstrates inability to sustain control systems for required transfer period; maintain monthly capacity tests until replacement.

summary: Operational performance is acceptable but several process and structural maintenance items require attention to maintain tertiary effluent standards. Recommended corrective actions should be scheduled in the near term to avoid further decline in treatment reliability.

findings.description: Concrete hairline cracking observed along the southwest wall of secondary clarifier #1, with crack widths ranging 0.2–0.6 mm and delamination pockets up to 25 mm diameter. Cracks are located at the waterline and extend vertically up to 1.2 m.
findings.recommendation: Perform epoxy injection sealing and localized patch repair within 180 days and schedule a follow-up structural survey in 12 months to monitor crack propagation.

findings.description: UV disinfection unit #3 exhibits reduced delivered UV dose; measured UV intensity at the effluent sleeve surface is 22 mW/cm2 compared with new-lamp baseline of 38 mW/cm2 and target minimum of 30 mW/cm2 for design flow at current UVT. Quartz sleeves show moderate fouling and 30% lamp aging (hours logged ~8,400).
findings.recommendation: Replace 50% of UV lamps and clean quartz sleeves within 30 days, then re-measure delivered dose; if intensity remains below 30 mW/cm2, schedule full bank replacement within 90 days.

findings.description: Mixed liquor suspended solids (MLSS) in aerobic basins measured 2,200 mg/L, below the optimal operational range of 3,000–4,500 mg/L for the activated sludge design at 120 MLD. SRT appears shortened based on observed effluent TSS spikes during peak flow events.
findings.recommendation: Adjust wasting strategy and increase aeration setpoints to raise MLSS into the 3,000–4,000 mg/L range over the next 60 days; perform weekly MLSS monitoring until stable.

findings.description: Effluent turbidity was measured at 0.65 NTU during inspection, marginally above the facility target of 0.5 NTU for tertiary filtration under normal load. Filtrate backwash frequency on filter train B appears reduced compared to design.
findings.recommendation: Review filtration backwash scheduling and increase backwash frequency for filter train B within 30 days; monitor effluent turbidity daily for two weeks after adjustments.

summary: Critical failures were identified that significantly compromise treatment effectiveness and pose immediate public health risk. Immediate remedial actions are required to restore disinfection and contain solids overflows before returning to service.

findings.description: UV disinfection bank A is non-operational; all lamps are dark and online UV intensity reads 0 mW/cm2 at the outlet for the entire channel. Control logs indicate an uncorrected ballast failure and absence of redundant bank initiation.
findings.recommendation: Bring an emergency portable disinfection system online and initiate emergency chlorination within 24 hours; repair or replace UV ballasts and lamps and verify delivered dose to meet 30 mW/cm2 minimum before resuming normal discharge.

findings.description: Severe pitting corrosion observed on the gate plate of outlet structure G4 (south outfall) with maximum pit depth measured at 12 mm and through-thickness loss at two localized areas. Gate stiffness is compromised and leakage past the seat measured at 15 L/min.
findings.recommendation: Take gate G4 out of service and replace the gate plate assembly within 7 days; conduct a full metal-loss survey of adjacent structures and apply cathodic protection where indicated.

findings.description: Overflow of primary sludge into raw influent channel from conveyor failure at the primary sludge handling building (bay 1) resulting in spillage across floor drains and temporary release into containment sump. Observed sludge accumulation ~0.5 m depth in the local containment pit.
findings.recommendation: Shut down affected conveyor and complete mechanical replacement or major repair within 7 days; mobilize cleanup crew to remove accumulated sludge and disinfect affected drainage within 48 hours to prevent secondary contamination.

findings.description: Emergency eyewash and shower station adjacent to the chemical dosing room are non-functional with blocked water supply and corroded nozzles. The chemical containment signage is faded and incomplete.
findings.recommendation: Restore eyewash/shower functionality within 24 hours and ensure full flow testing; replace signage and inspect all emergency safety stations facility-wide within 7 days.

summary: The Riverside Pump Station is in generally good operational condition with all core systems functional. Observed deficiencies are predominantly minor maintenance items and do not currently compromise station capacity, but several components require near-term service to prevent escalation.

findings.description: Vibration survey at pump 2 coupling measured 5.6 mm/s RMS (bearing housing), exceeding typical ISO 10816 guidance for smooth operation and indicating early bearing wear. Bearing temperature at the same unit was 68°C, approximately 18°C above the adjacent pump baseline.
findings.recommendation: Schedule bearing inspection and coupling alignment within 30 days and replace worn bearings within 90 days if corrective alignment does not reduce vibration to <3.5 mm/s.

findings.description: Diesel generator startup test showed extended cranking and battery terminal corrosion at the generator battery bank; measured open-circuit voltage 11.8 V under no-load condition. Corrosion on terminals has a light white/green deposit localized to positive posts.
findings.recommendation: Clean battery terminals and apply corrosion inhibitor immediately and replace generator battery within 30 days; verify reliable auto-start during monthly tests.

findings.description: Minor surface rust observed on the downstream face of the discharge manifold flanges and on sluice gate edges at the south intake, with coating delamination patches up to 40 mm in diameter. No through-wall loss detected on visual inspection.
findings.recommendation: Perform targeted abrasive cleaning and touch-up coating of affected flange faces and gate edges within 90 days and include these areas in the annual corrosion monitoring program.

findings.description: Measured combined pumping rate during normal operation was approximately 2,300 L/s (92% of rated 2,500 L/s capacity), with inlet screens free of major debris at time of inspection. Minor bypass flow observed during peak hours due to occasional screen clogging.
findings.recommendation: Maintain current debris-removal schedule and increase visual checks to weekly during high-flow seasons; schedule additional screen cleaning if combined output drops below 2,200 L/s.

summary: The pump station is functionally serviceable but exhibits multiple maintenance and repair needs that could impact reliability if not addressed. Several components show signs of deterioration requiring prioritized corrective action within the next 30–90 days.

findings.description: Concrete slab adjacent to pump 3 sump exhibits transverse cracks up to 0.8 mm wide extending 1.2 m along the north edge; hairline cracking has begun to propagate toward the access walkway. Cracking exceeds common superficial crack width thresholds of 0.3–0.5 mm used for preventative monitoring.
findings.recommendation: Seal cracks and install a bonded epoxy injection repair within 60 days, and schedule a structural review if further propagation is observed; monitor quarterly for new cracking or widening.

findings.description: Mechanical seal on pump 4 exhibiting steady leakage measured at approximately 0.5 L/min at the seal gland during operation, indicative of seal wear and potential shaft corrosion if allowed to continue. Leakage trails and minor fluid accumulation were observed at the pump base.
findings.recommendation: Replace the mechanical seal and inspect the shaft sleeve within 30 days and run a full operational test after replacement to confirm leak remediation.

findings.description: Control panel A (east control room) contained pockets of condensation on the door interior and evidence of moisture ingress at the lower cable gland entries; enclosures are rated NEMA 4 but gasket compression is uneven. Component terminals show early oxidation but remain functional.
findings.recommendation: Dry and reseal control panel A, replace compromised gaskets, and install a low-power dehumidifier in the control room within 30 days; retest panel ingress protection after corrective action.

findings.description: Sump level sensor calibration is inconsistent: manual verification showed the primary level transducer reads 40 mm lower than a calibrated reference float at the mid-sump elevation, causing delayed pump staging. Float-switch backup was observed to stick intermittently.
findings.recommendation: Recalibrate or replace the primary level transducer and service the float-switch assembly within 14 days, and perform operational verification of automatic staging immediately after service.

summary: The Riverside Pump Station exhibits significant operational deficiencies and safety risks that require immediate remediation. Critical systems, including backup power and multiple pumps, failed to meet operational standards during inspection and should be repaired or isolated without delay.

findings.description: Diesel generator failed to start during automatic and manual start attempts; starter engagement was absent and control panel displayed fault code indicating fuel lift pump failure. No reliable backup power is available, placing the station at risk during a mains outage.
findings.recommendation: Do not operate the station without corrective action; repair or replace the generator fuel lift pump and associated starter circuit within 7 days and perform a full functional load test before returning to service.

findings.description: Two of four pumps (pump 1 and pump 3) were offline: pump 1 has a fractured shaft coupling with 4 mm axial misalignment at the coupling face, and pump 3 exhibits seized bearing motion. Pump outages reduce available capacity by 50% compared to the station rated 2,500 L/s.
findings.recommendation: Replace the fractured coupling and perform full shaft alignment for pump 1 within 7 days; disassemble pump 3, replace seized bearings and inspect the rotor for damage within 14 days to restore full capacity.

findings.description: Severe localized corrosion and pitting observed on the discharge manifold at the northeast elbow, with measured pitting depth up to 6 mm and estimated local wall loss exceeding 30% of original wall thickness. Small through-wall leaks noted as weeping at flange seam under pressure test.
findings.recommendation: Isolate and replace the affected section of discharge manifold immediately; schedule replacement within 14 days and conduct pressure testing and non-destructive wall-thickness mapping on adjacent piping.

findings.description: Emergency stop buttons at the east and west pump bays were inoperable during functional test and the eyewash station in the control room lacked signage and had no water flow when activated. These deficiencies increase personnel risk during incidents.
findings.recommendation: Repair or replace emergency stop actuators and restore eyewash unit functionality within 7 days, and install appropriate signage; perform a site-wide safety equipment check after repairs.

summary: The Greenfield Booster Station is in generally good condition with all three pumps operational and the diesel backup generator functional under test load. Observed issues are limited to elevated vibration on one pump, superficial corrosion on the discharge manifold, and intermittent alarm log entries in the control panel. These issues are not currently critical but require scheduled maintenance to prevent escalation.

findings.description: Vibration measurement at the bearing housing of Pump 2 (northwestern pump bay) recorded 5.6 mm/s RMS, exceeding the ISO 10816 guideline for this class (~4.5 mm/s) and indicating bearing wear or misalignment. No excessive noise was present during inspection but vibration is consistently above recommended threshold under normal load.
findings.recommendation: Perform shaft alignment and dynamic balancing within 30 days and replace the bearing if vibration does not reduce below 4.5 mm/s; schedule vibration monitoring quarterly thereafter.

findings.description: Surface rust and localized pitting observed on the pump discharge manifold flange for Pump 1 (east discharge run), with pitting depths up to approximately 1.2 mm over a 150 mm length. Coating delamination is limited and no through-wall corrosion was detected.
findings.recommendation: Clean the affected area, remove loose rust, and apply an epoxy corrosion coating within 180 days; re-inspect coating adhesion and pitting annually.

findings.description: Control panel CP-1 (control room) operating temperature measured 38°C and within acceptable limits, but SCADA logs show intermittent non-critical alarm events with timestamps over the past 30 days. No fault codes preventing operation were present.
findings.recommendation: Update SCADA firmware and clear historical alarm logs within 60 days; implement monthly log reviews to verify alarms are resolved and to identify recurring faults.

summary: The booster station is functioning but shows several items requiring maintenance to restore design performance and ensure safety. Notable issues include reduced flow from one pump, fuel contamination in the diesel generator tank, and missing safety guarding. Repairs and corrective actions are recommended within 7 to 60 days depending on severity.

findings.description: Pump 1 discharge flow measured 220 L/s at FM-1 with a suction pressure of 1.8 bar, which is approximately 17.7% below expected single-pump capacity (~267 L/s based on 800 L/s total capacity divided by 3). The reduced flow suggests impeller wear, partial blockage, or hydraulic imbalance.
findings.recommendation: Inspect Pump 1 impeller and suction strainer within 30 days; clean or replace damaged impeller components and perform a pump performance test within 60 days to restore flow to design capacity.

findings.description: Diesel generator fuel tank (east tank, external) fuel sample contained ~0.8% free water by volume and visible sediment, exceeding acceptable limits (<0.1% water). During weekly start, generator ran but with irregular fuel pressure fluctuations.
findings.recommendation: Drain and replace contaminated fuel, perform fuel polishing and tank cleaning within 15 days, and re-test the generator under full load before returning it to standby service.

findings.description: The coupling guard between Pump 3 and its motor in the southern pump bay is missing, leaving rotating elements exposed and creating a risk of entanglement. No temporary barrier or signage was present.
findings.recommendation: Install a compliant coupling guard that meets OSHA/EN safety requirements within 7 days and perform a safety audit of guards on all rotating equipment.

findings.description: Floor drains in the pump bay are partially clogged; observed standing water up to 25 mm deep near Pump 2 after a 2-hour rainfall. Standing water increases slip risk and accelerates corrosion at baseplates.
findings.recommendation: Clear and flush floor drains and inspect sump pump operation within 30 days; implement quarterly maintenance checks of drainage during wet season.

summary: The Greenfield Booster Station exhibits multiple serious deficiencies that compromise reliability and safety of station operations. Active piping leaks, failed generator start and degraded structural anchors require immediate attention to prevent failure of pumping capability and potential environmental release. Immediate isolation of affected systems and expedited repairs are recommended.

findings.description: Active through-wall crack detected on the suction manifold elbow (northwest run) with an observed leak rate of approximately 10 L/min and wet staining of 150 x 50 mm; suction pressure fluctuates ±0.4 bar causing cavitation indications on Pump 2. Crack appears associated with corrosion fatigue at a welded seam.
findings.recommendation: Shut down and isolate Pump 2 immediately and restrict station capacity until repair is complete; replace the affected suction manifold section and associated flange hardware within 48 hours and perform a full hydraulic test before return to service.

findings.description: Backup diesel generator failed automatic start during test, achieving only ~60% of rated output and battery bank measured 40.0 V under load (nominal 48 V). Generator failed the manufacturer-specified automatic transfer test and did not sustain critical loads.
findings.recommendation: Do not rely on the generator for emergency power; replace or recharge starter battery bank and remediate generator starting/system faults within 24 hours and conduct a full load test before restoring to standby duty. Arrange temporary external power support if required.

findings.description: Severe corrosion observed at baseplate anchor bolts in the northeast corner of the motor base, with two anchors fractured and baseplate settlement measured at 12 mm relative to the original benchmark (acceptable <5 mm). Corrosion has progressed to loss of preload on anchors.
findings.recommendation: Install temporary shims and mechanical restraints immediately to stabilize the baseplate, and schedule replacement of fractured anchors and remedial foundation repairs within 14 days; consider full baseplate replacement if further settlement is found within 90 days.

findings.description: Fixed gas detector in control room CR-1 shows last calibration 28 months ago (exceeds the required annual calibration interval) and current readings are not certified. This presents a risk to personnel in the event of a gas release.
findings.recommendation: Calibrate or replace the gas detector within 7 days and establish a documented annual calibration schedule with verification logs.

summary: The inspected section of Harbor Seawall Section A is in generally good condition with localized deterioration at discrete locations. Minor concrete cracking and surface corrosion of sheet piling were observed, but no immediate structural failure indicators were present. Targeted repairs and routine monitoring are recommended to maintain serviceability in the marine environment.

findings.description: Longitudinal hairline cracks observed in the reinforced concrete facing between chainage 120 m and 180 m, with measured widths up to 1.2 mm and lengths of 2–6 m. Crack widths exceed the commonly accepted maximum of 1.0 mm for reinforced concrete in marine exposures and indicate early-stage chloride ingress risk.
findings.recommendation: Seal and epoxy-inject cracks in the affected zones and apply a surface moisture barrier; schedule repairs within 180 days and perform quarter‑ly monitoring of crack widths for 12 months.

findings.description: Steel sheet piling at chainage 200 m to 230 m shows surface corrosion concentrated at the tidal zone (approx. -0.5 m to +0.5 m elevation), with localized section loss up to approximately 8% based on caliper measurements. Corrosion rate is above expected background for coated piles but not yet compromising global capacity.
findings.recommendation: Mechanically clean affected areas, apply a marine-grade epoxy coating, and install sacrificial anodes if monitoring indicates progression; complete initial remediation within 120 days and re-inspect semi-annually.

findings.description: Several weep holes between chainage 50 m and 70 m are partially blocked by sediment and marine growth, resulting in shallow pooling (approx. 20 mm) behind the wall after tide cycles. Reduced drainage increases saturation of the retained material but is currently minor and localized.
findings.recommendation: Clear and flush the weep holes and verify flow restoration; perform monthly inspections for six months and, if clogging recurs, replace affected weep holes within 90 days.

summary: Harbor Seawall Section A exhibits fair condition with multiple areas requiring maintenance to prevent progressive deterioration. Notable spalling, moderate sheet pile corrosion near mean sea level, and toe scour were observed and should be addressed to avoid escalation. A prioritized repair program and enhanced monitoring are advised.

findings.description: Top coping spalling observed between chainage 300 m and 320 m, exposing approximately 0.5 m² of reinforcing steel with concrete cover loss on the order of 20 mm. Chloride staining present at the exposed reinforcement indicates ongoing corrosion risk compared to the design cover (≈50 mm) recommended for marine structures.
findings.recommendation: Remove loose concrete, clean and treat exposed reinforcement, and patch with marine-grade repair mortar; schedule repairs within 90 days and monitor the patched area quarterly for re-corrosion.

findings.description: Sheet piles at chainage 330 m to 360 m exhibit pitting and localized section loss up to 20% at mean sea level, with pits measured to a depth of approximately 12 mm. Pitting at this severity reduces fatigue life and may accelerate through‑thickness penetration if not mitigated.
findings.recommendation: Perform a cathodic protection feasibility study within 60 days and proceed with either CP installation or selective pile replacement for the worst-affected panels within 180 days.

findings.description: Toe scour identified at the seaward base between chainage 210 m and 230 m with approximately 0.35 m of vertical exposure of sheet pile and partial undermining of the toe mattress. Scour extent exceeds normal allowable exposure for the toe protection system and threatens local stability.
findings.recommendation: Install rock armor or geotextile mattress at the toe to re-establish protection and prevent further undermining; implement this work within 90 days and inspect monthly until stabilized.

findings.description: Protective coating on the top-of-wall surfaces and handrails is flaking and peeling over roughly the northmost 50 m, exposing substrate to accelerated degradation. Remaining coating in adjacent areas shows diminished adhesion but no through‑section damage yet.
findings.recommendation: Recoat the exposed areas with a marine-grade coating system and schedule a full top-of-wall recoating within 12 months; inspect coatings annually thereafter.

summary: Significant deterioration and active damage were observed at a localized portion of Harbor Seawall Section A, posing an elevated risk to structural integrity and public safety. Advanced sheet pile corrosion, large vertical cracks with displacement, and active seepage require immediate intervention. Emergency stabilization and expedited replacement of affected components are recommended.

findings.description: Multiple vertical cracks at construction joints between chainage 120 m and 140 m with measured widths up to 10 mm and observed horizontal displacement of 30–50 mm; adjacent backfill shows localized settlement of approximately 50 mm and wall rotation towards the water. These deformations exceed allowable serviceability limits and indicate loss of structural continuity.
findings.recommendation: Restrict public access adjacent to the affected section immediately and perform emergency stabilization (shoring or temporary bracing) within 7 days; retain a structural engineering firm to perform a detailed assessment and produce a remedial design within 30 days.

findings.description: Steel sheet piles in the same zone (chainage 125 m to 155 m) show advanced generalized corrosion with localized section loss exceeding 35% and multiple perforations at mean low water, permitting direct seawater ingress into the retained soil. Through‑thickness loss is evident on both faces of several interlocked sheets.
findings.recommendation: Execute emergency replacement of the severely affected sheet pile panels and install temporary bracing within 30 days; plan and budget for full replacement of the top 50 m of seawall in this zone within 6 months if structural analysis confirms loss of capacity.

findings.description: Active freshwater seepage and tidal leakage observed through vertical cracks and at the base of the wall in the 120 m–160 m zone, with a measured discharge of approximately 3 L/min at one outlet. Sustained seepage has increased pore pressures in the retained soil and is contributing to the observed settlement.
findings.recommendation: Install temporary dewatering measures (sumps/sandbags) immediately and assess permanent drainage repairs (relief wells or new weeps) to be implemented within 60 days.

findings.description: Top railing anchors between chainage 130 m and 132 m have lost fixation and the adjacent walkway exhibits a 40 mm displacement, creating an immediate trip and fall hazard for pedestrians. Handrail instability was confirmed by physical testing.
findings.recommendation: Cordon off and post hazard signage at the walkway within 24 hours and prohibit access until repairs are made; remove and replace railing anchors and repair the walkway surfacing within 30 days.

summary: The Meridian Cut Retaining Wall generally performs well with only localized, repairable deficiencies. Issues observed are typical for a mechanically stabilized earth wall of this age and do not threaten overall stability if addressed in a timely manner. Routine maintenance and monitoring are recommended to prevent progression.

findings.description: Localized facing separations and minor differential rotation were observed between chainage 120 m and 140 m, with measured horizontal offsets ranging from 15 mm to 30 mm (typical ~20 mm). These displacements exceed typical manufacturer installation tolerances (≈25 mm) in isolated locations but do not indicate active global failure.
findings.recommendation: Seal and re-fasten facing panels and adjust anchors where required; schedule corrective work within 180 days and monitor the zone quarterly for progressive movement.

findings.description: Weep holes and the toe discharge at chainage 60 m to 75 m are partially clogged with silt and organic debris, reducing observed discharge by an estimated 40% compared to adjacent clear sections. Reduced drainage increases hydrostatic loading risk behind the wall.
findings.recommendation: Clean all weep holes and the exposed toe outlet and install a geotextile filter at the discharge points; complete cleaning and verification within 60 days and re-inspect after the next significant rainfall event.

findings.description: Volunteer vegetation (small shrubs and grasses) is present along the facing at several locations (notably 10 m–20 m intervals), with root intrusion beginning to contact facing joints. Vegetation is shallow-rooted and not currently compromising reinforcement.
findings.recommendation: Remove invasive vegetation and apply an approved herbicidal treatment; schedule removal within 90 days and implement annual vegetation control during routine inspections.

summary: The wall exhibits several performance issues requiring planned maintenance and targeted repairs. Observed settlements, seepage, and localized corrosion indicate moderate degradation of components and drainage function. Repairs should be scheduled and prioritized to prevent escalation to structural distress.

findings.description: Differential settlement and outward bulging observed between chainage 200 m and 215 m with vertical settlement up to 50 mm and outward facing displacement measured at approximately 80 mm. The deformation pattern suggests localized loss of compaction or reinforcement pullout in the reinforced zone.
findings.recommendation: Perform geotechnical evaluation and remove/recompact selected backfill; replace or extend reinforcement (geogrid) in the affected 15 m segment and complete repairs within 30 days to restore design geometry.

findings.description: Active seepage with staining and efflorescence observed at chainage 45 m to 50 m. Measured seepage flow is approximately 0.4–0.6 L/min at the outlet during inspection, indicating a partially clogged subsurface drain and elevated pore pressures behind the wall.
findings.recommendation: Excavate to expose the drainage outlet, clean or replace the subdrain (perforated pipe and adjacent filter material), and install an improved outlet with inspection access; schedule work within 30 days and verify reduced seepage after repair.

findings.description: Exposed metallic reinforcement strips and anchor plates at chainage 210 m to 230 m show surface rust and areas of zinc coating loss; pitting corrosion observed at several anchor terminations with estimated coating loss of 20–30%.
findings.recommendation: Remove loose corrosion products, apply corrosion inhibitor and protective coating to minor-affected elements, and plan full replacement of heavily corroded anchors within 180 days. Inspect adjacent anchors systemically for similar deterioration.

findings.description: Hairline cracks (≤3 mm) observed in the concrete coping along chainage 0 m to 10 m with minor water staining; cracking appears to be surface shrinkage without active movement.
findings.recommendation: Clean and seal coping cracks with low-modulus sealant to prevent ingress; complete sealing within 90 days and re-evaluate during the next annual inspection.

summary: Significant performance deficiencies were observed that compromise the retaining wall's stability in multiple locations. Conditions include extensive facing deformation, active undermining at the toe, clogged drainage, and advanced corrosion of reinforcement. Immediate stabilization and prioritized repairs are required to mitigate risk to adjacent infrastructure and public safety.

findings.description: Severe outward bulging and rotation of the wall facing detected between chainage 140 m and 170 m with maximum measured horizontal displacement of 250 mm and rotation of facing units up to 6°. Deformation is concentrated over a 30 m length and indicates loss of internal reinforcement capacity.
findings.recommendation: Implement emergency shoring and remove public access adjacent to the affected zone immediately (within 24–48 hours). Complete full replacement of the affected 30 m wall segment, including new reinforcement and facing, within 30 days.

findings.description: Primary subsurface drainage is fully clogged along a continuous run from 240 m to 280 m with standing water observed at the base and elevated pore pressures inferred. Clogging has resulted in pronounced hydrostatic pressure and active toe instability.
findings.recommendation: Excavate and replace the drainage blanket and perforated collector pipe with a geotextile-wrapped system; complete emergency drainage remediation within 14 days and install permanent inspection points.

findings.description: Undermining and scour at the toe were observed from chainage 250 m to 275 m, with a 25 m length showing 150–200 mm loss of support and localized slump of the facing and toe fill. The undermining appears linked to surface runoff concentration and blocked outlets.
findings.recommendation: Conduct immediate emergency stabilization (temporary underpinning, toe backfill, and erosion control) within 7 days. Plan design-level reconstruction of toe and adjacent drainage within 30 days.

findings.description: Anchor plates and exposed metallic reinforcement in the mid-wall zone (150 m–175 m) show advanced corrosion with estimated cross-sectional loss exceeding 50% at several terminations and pitting consistent with prolonged water exposure. Several anchors have significant section loss impacting pullout capacity.
findings.recommendation: Replace corroded anchors and reinforcement in the affected 25 m zone; perform a full inventory of all anchors along the wall and schedule replacement of those exceeding 30% loss within 60 days. Consider cathodic or protective systems for future mitigation.

findings.description: Top-of-wall barrier and pedestrian walkway adjacent to chainage 160 m exhibit failure of handrail anchorage and accumulated debris, creating immediate safety hazards for the public. There is no temporary barrier in place at the most deformed sections.
findings.recommendation: Install temporary barricades and signage to restrict access within 24 hours and repair/replace permanent handrails and walkway surface after structural stabilization; complete safety remediation within 7 days.

summary: Northern Reservoir is in generally good condition with the floating cover system intact and no evidence of significant structural settlement at the time of inspection. Minor coating deterioration and localized wear of cover anchor points were observed and should be addressed to prevent escalation. Routine monitoring and scheduled repairs will maintain serviceability and comply with standard maintenance intervals.

findings.description: Epoxy topcoat on the interior access ladder at the west embankment shows 10-15% area with light blistering and chalking approximately 1.5 m above the waterline. The underlying metal remains sound with no pitting observed.
findings.recommendation: Remove blisters, perform spot-sanding and apply a full epoxy touch-up coating within 90 days to restore protective performance per AWWA guidance.

findings.description: Floating cover anchor assemblies in the northwest quadrant (stations 115-125) show wear with two anchors exhibiting elongated bolt holes (~4 mm elongation). Tensioning cables maintain cover position but exhibit increased free-play.
findings.recommendation: Replace elongated anchor bolts and retension cables within 30 days and inspect remaining anchors quarterly for progressive wear.

findings.description: Perimeter safety signage along the north access road is faded and two sections of chain-link fence have loose top rails 6 m and 18 m from the spillway. Current signage does not meet legibility standards for emergency responders.
findings.recommendation: Replace faded signage and secure fence rails within 60 days and include signage verification in biannual safety inspections.

summary: The reservoir exhibits fair condition with multiple items requiring maintenance to prevent further deterioration. Notable issues include active seepage at the north abutment and vegetation encroachment on the west embankment compromising toe drainage. Targeted repairs and a maintenance program are recommended to restore reliability and prevent loss of capacity.

findings.description: Active seepage observed at the north abutment (approx. 2.3 L/min estimated from visual staining and damp soil) emanating from a 0.5 m wide saturated zone 4 m below crest elevation. Seepage has created a soft spot in the perimeter walking path approximately 6 m from the reservoir wall.
findings.recommendation: Conduct detailed geotechnical evaluation within 14 days and implement seepage control measures (grouting or toe drain rehabilitation) within 60 days to prevent slope instability.

findings.description: Floating cover underside displays localized biofilm and algal buildup covering approximately 8% of the surface, concentrated near the inlet bay. The buildup is reducing drainage efficiency and could increase organic load in stored water.
findings.recommendation: Schedule mechanical cleaning of the cover underside and inlet bay within 30 days and institute quarterly cleanings during warm months to control regrowth.

findings.description: Minor settlement cracks (up to 12 mm wide) observed along the paved inspection road adjacent to the south crest; no vertical misalignment greater than 25 mm detected in wall panels. Cracks are allowing surface water ingress toward the embankment core.
findings.recommendation: Seal roadway cracks and perform monitoring of crack width monthly for six months; if widening exceeds 5 mm/month, schedule structural assessment within 30 days.

findings.description: Emergency access gate at the east entrance is partially obstructed by overgrown vegetation, limiting vehicular access in an emergency. Fire department clearance of 3 m was not met in one location.
findings.recommendation: Trim vegetation to restore full access within 15 days and add vegetation control to the quarterly maintenance checklist.

summary: Significant deficiencies were observed that compromise the reservoir's integrity and operational safety. A large breach in the floating cover and compromised anchor system have created uncontrolled leakage and increased risk of contamination; immediate remedial action is required to limit water loss and prevent further structural damage.

findings.description: A 2.2 m2 tear was measured in the floating cover in the southeast quadrant adjacent to the outlet bay, with cover material delaminated and water ingress evident through the breach. The tear corresponds with torn reinforcement stitching and two adjacent tension cables have failed.
findings.recommendation: Isolate the affected cover section and perform emergency patch or replace the damaged cover panel within 7 days; replace failed tension cables and inspect entire cable network immediately after repair.

findings.description: Anchor pad plates and bolts around the perimeter (particularly between stations 40-60 on the east side) exhibit advanced galvanic corrosion with flange loss estimated at 30-40% cross-section and active rust staining. Several anchor bolts have reduced thread engagement.
findings.recommendation: Replace severely corroded anchor plates and bolts within 30 days and apply corrosion-inhibiting coatings; institute semiannual anchor inspections thereafter.

findings.description: Outlet chamber access hatch shows water infiltration and sand/grit accumulation reducing outlet gate operability; manual gate operation required force exceeding 250 N during test. Reduced gate function is increasing risk of uncontrolled discharge during high-demand events.
findings.recommendation: Clear inlet chamber debris, restore drainage to outlet gate, and perform gate maintenance or replacement within 14 days to ensure full operational control.

findings.description: Temporary floating debris and plastic sheeting trapped against the damaged cover present contamination and entrapment hazards near the operator walkway. Confined-space entry signage is absent at the outlet chamber access point.
findings.recommendation: Remove debris immediately and install compliant confined-space signage and temporary barriers within 3 days; conduct PPE briefing for maintenance crews prior to work on the cover.

summary: The Greenfield Solar Array is performing well with only minor issues affecting long-term reliability. Most modules and racking remain in good condition, and inverters are operating within expected parameters. Targeted maintenance actions are recommended to prevent escalation of observed minor defects.

findings.description: String inverter #2 (pad 02, east equipment row) exhibited a 3.4% reduction in AC output compared to baseline performance records for the previous 12 months, likely related to elevated DC input ripple observed at 11:00 local time. Measured DC ripple and harmonics are within safe limits for immediate operation but indicate declining MPPT stability.
findings.recommendation: Schedule inverter firmware update and full string-level IV curve test within 30 days; if output does not recover within 14 days post-update, plan inverter servicing or replacement within 90 days.

findings.description: Aluminum racking paint on rows R3–R6 (northwest quadrant) shows localized UV degradation and chalking over approximately 2% of the surface area, with no current structural compromise. This is consistent with expected lifetime exposure but will reduce corrosion protection over time.
findings.recommendation: Apply touch-up coating to affected racking within 180 days and include coated sample panels for adhesion testing during next annual inspection to ensure long-term protection.

findings.description: Two access gates (south perimeter) have self-closing mechanisms with reduced tension, allowing gates to remain open intermittently. This presents a minor unauthorized-access risk but no immediate electrical hazard.
findings.recommendation: Adjust or replace gate closers and confirm self-closing operation within 30 days; document repairs and verify perimeter security monthly for three months.

summary: The solar array exhibits signs of moderate degradation that require scheduled maintenance to restore performance and mitigate future failures. Several electrical and structural issues were observed that, if unaddressed, could lead to increased downtime and reduced energy yield. A prioritized maintenance program should be implemented within the next 90 days.

findings.description: Random string-level insulation resistance tests across inverters 4–7 returned values between 0.5 and 0.9 MΩ at 1000 VDC, which is below the manufacturer-recommended minimum of 1.0 MΩ and below typical industry practice (>1.5 MΩ). This indicates moisture ingress or degradation of module junction box seals in the central array section (rows R20–R28).
findings.recommendation: Perform immediate detailed IR thermography and wet/dry insulation testing within 14 days; schedule replacement of affected junction boxes and re-sealing of modules within 60 days to restore insulation resistance above 1.5 MΩ.

findings.description: Multiple mid-span racking bolts (rows R10 and R11, east side) were found with torque loss of 20–35% relative to design torque during manual verification, and one splice plate showed a hairline crack approximately 18 mm long. No gross displacement was observed but loss of clamping increases fatigue risk.
findings.recommendation: Retighten or replace torqued bolts and replace the cracked splice plate within 30 days; implement quarterly torque audits for the next year to ensure stability.

findings.description: Surface water pooling observed along access aisle A between rows R14–R16 after recent rains, with puddles up to 40 mm deep and localized detritus obstructing flow paths. Continued pooling increases soiling rates and raises corrosion risk at lower module frames.
findings.recommendation: Clear drainage channels and regrade the access aisle to eliminate pooling within 45 days; install additional gravel-filled swales if pooling reoccurs during the next storm season.

findings.description: Warning labels on combiner boxes C-12 and C-13 are faded and partially illegible, reducing clarity of high-voltage hazard information for maintenance personnel.
findings.recommendation: Replace faded safety labels on affected combiner boxes within 30 days and include label condition checks in monthly maintenance rounds.

summary: The Greenfield Solar Array is in poor condition with multiple critical deficiencies requiring immediate intervention. Significant electrical safety risks and structural corrosion were identified; continued operation without expedited remediation presents an unacceptable risk. Immediate shutdown of affected sections and emergency repairs are recommended.

findings.description: Exposed DC conductors found at five string junction boxes (rows R5–R7, south cluster) with damaged insulation and visible copper strands; IR imaging shows hot spots up to 85°C at these locations under nominal load. Measured leakage current on one affected string was 12 mA with insulation resistance of 0.25 MΩ at 1000 VDC.
findings.recommendation: Isolate and de-energize affected strings immediately; schedule emergency replacement of damaged conductors and junction boxes within 7 days and re-test insulation to confirm >1.5 MΩ before re-commissioning.

findings.description: Structural anchor bolts at the northwest perimeter (anchors A1–A6) exhibit advanced galvanic corrosion with section loss up to 30% and pit depths measured up to 2.4 mm. Two anchors show measurable movement under load testing, compromising array anchorage.
findings.recommendation: Conduct immediate load-rated shoring of the affected racking and replace corroded anchors within 14 days; perform full anchor survey across the site within 30 days to identify any additional at-risk components.

findings.description: Multiple module frames in rows R1–R4 show evidence of delamination and microcracking (approx. 0.5–1.5 mm hairline cracks) at module edges, and three modules exhibited glass separation creating ingress paths. Mechanical stability of affected modules is compromised under wind loading.
findings.recommendation: Remove and replace all visibly delaminated or cracked modules within 21 days and inspect adjacent modules for propagation; retain replaced modules for manufacturer warranty claim processing.

findings.description: The inverter room ventilation fan 1 is non-functional, resulting in ambient temperatures exceeding the inverter safe operating limit by 12°C during midday peak, increasing risk of inverter failure and fire. Additionally, the AC distribution panel cover at inverter bay 3 is missing, exposing live connections.
findings.recommendation: Shut down inverter bay 3 immediately until the AC panel cover is reinstalled and secure ventilation is restored; replace fan and verify ventilation within 72 hours and repair or replace AC panel cover within 48 hours, then conduct safety re-certification before returning to service.
